scores.materiality_reasoning: Freedom from state-mandated 'supervision' has a direct effect on the legal and physical autonomy of elite individuals.
scores.generality_reasoning: State supervision is a broad category of social and legal control over a large segment of the population.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Exemption is structural and lasts for the duration of one's life as a member of the elite class.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Defined by sex and wealth/status of the household, which are fixed birth or structural traits.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aristotle is analyzing contemporary Greek political structures and their existing administrative offices.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reported as a logical consequence of political forms (aristocracy vs. democracy) observed by the author.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being 'rich' or 'female' does not inherently define one as 'exempt from state superintendents'.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The power to veto legislation is a concrete legal right with direct material effect on state policy.
scores.generality_reasoning: Legislative rejection power is a foundational societal axis regarding political sovereignty.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Holds as long as the individual retains the legal status of a 'common' citizen within that constitution.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Commoner status is a legal birth-status, difficult to change except through significant wealth gain or exile.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aristotle refers to constitutional designs in use or proposed for use in the Greek world of his time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim uses the word 'ought' (dei), framing it as a normative proposal for better governance.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being 'common people' does not definitionally include the legal right to reject measures.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Supreme control over laws and hereditary office constitutes total political and economic power.
scores.generality_reasoning: Political power and control over the legal framework are foundational societal axes.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Hereditary status is lifelong and passes to descendants, creating a permanent structural hierarchy.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Nobility/heredity is biologically or birth-fixed and strictly immutable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Aristotle describes a specific type of oligarchical system active in his contemporary political landscape.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: States the functional outcome of hereditary systems as an analytical fact of political science.
scores.tautology_reasoning: While nobles often hold power, 'noble' refers to lineage, not inherently 'supreme legal control'.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Eligibility for the judiciary confers significant legal power and economic influence over trials/fines.
scores.generality_reasoning: Judicial service in Athens and other poleis was a broad, foundational political right.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Eligibility is a permanent feature of citizen status from adulthood until death.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a structural birth-status that is very hard to change.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the standard 'popular' judicial model practiced in existing fourth-century democracies like Athens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The text classifies existing modes of court composition as a systematic descriptive fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Participating in courts is a specific right of citizenship, not its definition.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Absolute equality involves shared access to property, legal protections, and political power.
scores.generality_reasoning: Participation in 'all things' is the broadest possible category of life outcomes.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Democratic citizenship and its attendant equality are treated as a lifelong structural right.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizen status based on free birth is a hard-to-change legal identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Analyzes the prevailing ideology and current functioning of democratic regimes of his time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Mixed: describes the real psychological/legal basis of a group's claim while explaining their ideology.
scores.tautology_reasoning: The rule as phrased (equal people participate equally) is essentially a restatement of the group's own claim.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Political power in an oligarchy directly translates to wealth protection and legislative control.
scores.generality_reasoning: Political power is a foundational axis of societal organization.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Office and power in oligarchies are often long-term or structural based on property.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth brackets are changeable with effort or fortune, though often stable over a lifetime.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the structural mechanics of contemporary oligarchic regimes.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: An analytical description of how oligarchic power is distributed in reality.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Wealth does not inherently mean political power in all systems, only in oligarchic ones.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The right to unequal status includes specific legal exemptions and political privileges.
scores.generality_reasoning: Unequal (superior) rights cover a broad class of social and legal life outcomes.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Ancestral status is hereditary and persists throughout the individual's entire life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Lineage and 'ancestral virtue' are birth-fixed and strictly immutable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the persistent claims and social rules of noble families in fourth-century Greece.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reported as a social fact of how certain groups claim and exert their status.
scores.tautology_reasoning: While nobility implies status, specific 'unequal rights' are a legal addition to birth birth.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Attendance at the assembly is a social/political role with indirect material outcomes.
scores.generality_reasoning: Tied to a specific city (Epidamnus) and a specific type of assembly session.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Persistence lasts for the term of the magistracy, a significant but bounded period.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Magistrates are office-holders during a term; it is an episodic role.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the specific, ongoing constitutional rules in Epidamnus.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports a specific compulsory practice in a named city as a plain fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Magistrate status does not naturally entail 'exclusive attendance' at assemblies.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Banishment involves loss of land, home, and physical presence in the state.
scores.generality_reasoning: A specific legal procedure (ostracism) that removes a person from society.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The exile is described as 'temporary' (usually 10 years in the cases cited).
scores.immutability_reasoning: Being a 'notable' is a durable wealth/social identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Ostracism was a well-known contemporary practice in Athens and Argos during Aristotle's time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites 'custom' and specific cities to attest to a factual legal practice.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Banishment is not part of the definition of being a prominent citizen.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Exposure to frontline combat involves direct risk of death or physical injury.
scores.generality_reasoning: Military service is a major recurring interaction between citizen and state.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Bounded to the duration of the war or the period of military service.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The 'notables' are defined by social/wealth status, which is durable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers specifically to events during the Peloponnesian War, roughly a century prior.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the historical mechanics of the Athenian muster-roll as historical fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Notable status does not inherently imply exemption from the muster-roll.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to hold office provides professional status and direct influence over state resources.
scores.generality_reasoning: Eligibility for office is a broad category of political participation.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Property qualifications create a lifelong barrier to office for the poor.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth status ('poor') is a durable but potentially changeable social identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The specific example of Ambracia refers to historical development over several stages.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes property qualifications as a factual institutional mechanism.
scores.tautology_reasoning: The inability to hold office is a legal consequence of poverty, not its definition.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to remain reflects protection from displacement, which involves land and livelihood.
scores.generality_reasoning: Exclusion and expulsion are broad, high-consequence life outcomes.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Expulsion is typically a permanent loss of home and belonging.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Ethnic/geographic origin (Achaean, Troezenian) is strictly immutable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes historical founding events and subsequent conflicts in Sybaris and Thurii.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: The verbatim reports historical episodes as objective case studies in civil strife.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a secondary settler does not definitionally include being expelled.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Marriage in this context is inextricably tied to property (the daughters are 'heiresses').
scores.generality_reasoning: Control over marriage partner is a broad right affecting life, family, and wealth.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Marriage in the Greek world was a permanent-till-death structural status for women.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Defined by being female and a daughter, which is birth-fixed.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to historical events leading to the war with Athens under Paches.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Narrates the historical causes of a factional dispute based on legal marriage norms.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being an heiress does not definitionally mean having the right to choose a partner (rather, the opposite).

scores.materiality_reasoning: Confiscation and expulsion remove tangible land, homes, and money.
scores.generality_reasoning: Right to retain property and habitat is a foundational societal axis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Confiscation of property and expulsion are permanent or long-term structural changes.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The notables are defined by a wealth and status bracket.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to a specific historical revolution in Megara that led to an oligarchy.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports historical political events as factual evidence for how revolutions happen.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a notable does not definitionally mean being expelled or losing property.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Public services and confiscations directly drain liquid and fixed wealth.
scores.generality_reasoning: Protection of wealth from state extraction is a major category of rights.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Liturgies and taxes are recurring; confiscations are permanent.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealthy status is a durable, though potentially changeable, identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes common practices in contemporary Greek democracies known to the author.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reported as a standard factual occurrence in democratic polities.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being rich does not definitionally imply that one's estate will be divided.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Access to high office provides direct legal power and management of state wealth.
scores.generality_reasoning: Eligibility for high office is a foundational axis of political organization.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The removal of property assessments is a permanent structural shift in law.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The wealthy are a durable social group based on property ownership.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Analyzes contemporary shifts in democratic constitutional standards.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the objective structural transition from elective to radical democracy.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Exclusivity in office is a legal privilege, not a definition of being wealthy.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The right to hold office constitutes legal power and social/material influence.
scores.generality_reasoning: Exclusion from the magistracy is a broad political restriction.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Bounded to a life stage; younger brothers eventually become older ones.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Birth order (younger brother) and kinship relationship (son) are immutable birth facts.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Notes specific rules that exist ('in some places') in the current Greek world.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Attests to specific legal prohibitions regarding simultaneous office-holding as fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a younger brother does not definitionally exclude one from office.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Membership in a life-serving council of Elders confers supreme political and legal authority.
scores.generality_reasoning: Very specific to the council of the Elders (Gerousia) in Elis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The office is explicitly described as being held 'for life'.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Minor oligarchs are part of a durable, though not strictly birth-fixed, political class.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to a specific institutional history in Elis ('what once occurred').
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports the specific numerical and temporal rules of the Elean council as historical fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being part of a citizen class does not definitionally include or exclude one from high office.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Membership in councils and courts grants direct legal power and institutional influence.
scores.generality_reasoning: Participation in central political and judicial bodies is a foundational societal axis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Property-based exclusion acts as a lifelong barrier for those without sufficient wealth.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Poverty/wealth status is durable but not immutable.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes standard mechanisms used in modern oligarchies and polities.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Analyzes the factual operation of property qualifications in constitutional law.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Poverty does not definitionally imply exclusion from law courts, though it does in this rule.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Honors (offices/rights) at Thurii provided legal status and political authority.
scores.generality_reasoning: Access to state honors/offices is a broad category of political life.
scores.persistence_reasoning: High property qualifications created a structural, long-term barrier to participation.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth/property status is a durable identity.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Narrates a specific historical sequence of constitutional change and illegality at Thurii.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes historical constitutional thresholds and land-grabbing as factual events.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Access to 'honors' is a legal rule, not part of the definition of the poor.
